titleStmt.title: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to Elizabeth Tyrrell 1, November, 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Upton Tyrrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to his mother. £20 enclosed, £10 for a canon and £10 for a birthday present for Bobbie.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
81/11/17 My dear mammy
I was delighted to get your long
letter yesterday. I have not got any
of the parcels yet. I suppose they
must have been held up by the
storm. It is too bad because the
pheasants will be rotten I am afraid.
However 'tis the fortune of war and
of course game should be high
before eaten . They will all come
without a doubt . Every ones parals
have been held up the same way.
Enclosed is a cheque £1 - 0.0
for the Canon. I forgot all about it
till you mentioned it yesterday
but of course I will give it .
I am living in luxury here. I have
a nice dug out all to myself and have
all my kit up so am very comfortable .
The C.O orders me to bed every night
as soon as dinner is over and I
cannot say Ah! C.O. just another
5 minutes!!! I was sort of
offended at first at being ordered
to bed but last night he ordered
the major to go to bed too and he
had to go. I have nothing at all to do. I
just go and pay visits to my
friends when I feel inclined and
keep out of the way for the remainder
of the time so as not to show
to plainly that I have nothing to
do - I am adding another 10/-
to the cheque for you to get
something for Bobbie for his
birthday. Get him anything you
or he likes. I can't' suggest
anything. No news at all. The weather is
fine and the trenches dry so
nothing else matters at present
The apples did not come as yet but we
had apples and nuts galore last
night and a nice stove to roast the
chestnuts. Wasn't that great. I only
wish you could see us here and
you would never worry. We are
just as happy and comfortable as
we could wish to be. Rather an
amusing thing happened yeserday as
we were coming up the road. We have
a new officer named Phillips. He
was doing something wrong and the
C.O. yelled at him Can you read?
the officer said No sir: I'm Phillips Best love to all from
Your most affec Son
Willie
